summary: Justin Brennan coaches multifamily investors on deal underwriting fundamentals, focusing on analyzing a 10-unit Idaho property and a 9-unit Kansas City deal. He teaches how to work with incomplete financials, apply reasonable expense ratios by property age, use cap rates to determine valuations, structure offers, and understand financing mechanics including bridge debt and debt service coverage ratios.
takeaways: Use 40-50% expense ratio for properties built 1958-1960 when seller lacks documented expenses; verify with utility invoices and tax/insurance bills
Apply 6% cap rate minimum for Midwest deals outside coastal zones; cap rate must approximate interest rate (currently 5.8%-7.2%) or deal won't cash flow
For 10-unit deal: $1,000 current rent + $150 market rent bump = $57K to $65,550 NOI annually, raising valuation from $950K to $1.1M using 6% cap rate
Debt service coverage ratio must be minimum 1.25x; mortgage payment cannot exceed NOI divided by 1.25 (e.g., $57K NOI allows max $45,600 annual debt)
Bridge debt financing: short-term (2-year) variable rate loans covering purchase + renovation, designed to refi into fixed-rate debt after property stabilizes at higher rents
Only three deal underwriting levers move numbers: (1) revenue/rent growth, (2) expense reduction, (3) cheaper capital; if speculatively relying on hope-and-pray rent assumptions, pass on deal
Syndication fee structure: for 10-unit at $1.1M purchase, add $50K renovation + $70K closing costs = $1.2M total cost; acquire 2-3% ($20-30K) as acquisition fee from capital raise
Plug all scenarios into deal analyzer—static napkin math confirms loan coverage but doesn't prove 14-20% IRR or 6-10% cash-on-cash needed to attract OPM investors

skill_candidates.change: Apply consistent 40-50% expense ratios by property vintage when audited financials unavailable, verified against actual tax/insurance/utility invoices
skill_candidates.evidence: Robbie's 1958 Idaho property: Justin prescribed 50% baseline, reduced to 48% after Robbie provided utility bills and property tax/insurance docs; explicitly stated 'unless they can show you actual numbers, you have to use projections'

skill_candidates.change: Use cap rate minimum of 6% for Midwest properties to ensure debt service coverage; reject deals where cap rate falls below interest rate or requires >0.5% margin
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin told Rory: 'I would not use anything less than that [6% cap rate]. It's just not...you'd have to really make a good case' for Midwest; explained cap rate must approximate interest rate or property won't cover mortgage: 'If cap...is 5% yet you're paying 6.5% interest, you might as well bankrupt yourself'

skill_candidates.change: Calculate maximum allowable mortgage payment using 1.25x debt service coverage ratio before underwriting deal; reject if NOI cannot support 1.25 DCR at projected interest rate
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin coached Robbie: 'Your mortgage payment can't exceed $45,600 annually' based on $57K NOI ÷ 1.25 DCR; instructed 'you got to back into it to make sure your debt coverage ratio is at least 1.25' and verify with lender requirements

skill_candidates.change: Conduct in-person broker reconnaissance mission (3-day trip) in target market to build rapport and access off-market deal flow; relationship precedes deal access
skill_candidates.evidence: Bruce said Huntsville brokers aren't sharing off-market deals; Justin replied: 'You need to meet the brokers...they don't know you...spend a little bit of time, call it the reconnaissance mission...belly to belly...Put a face with a name...then [they'll] send you this deal that's coming to market'

title: Weekly Coaching_05-22-25 | Justin Brennan
